text:p.text:span: 20. x x lugal x di-ne gá-e ù-x-du-x
saying (the words) of Aradmu, your servant
When my king dispatched me to … the task,
the generals, lieutenants, and captains of my king’s far-flung troops
inquired about the health of my king;
and shouted out ‘Long Live the King!’”
When I was close to the work(-site),
after I inspected the work that my king had sent me to,
the one guilty of a capital offence was handed over for execution,
the sentenced had to bear their punishment.
The one who doubled his work-(load) was very well paid,
his wages were paid out in silver for each day.
Abaindasa, the captain of the royal armed forces,
who had been returned to the ranks by my king,
abandoned the task (assigned by) my king and rushed to work building fortifications.
My king, because the orders were not being …
but because he rebelled ... but could not send my messenger
He destroyed/broke … and had to bear punishment
and was ousted from that troop.
That crime was a very serious crime against my king!
After he was entrusted to a second man
Whatever my king orders me, I will do.
My king should know (about all this).
From this very [day] on, I will never neglect my king’s orders!

This is a preliminary edition. The transliteration was made from photographs; in November of 2005 I was able to make collations but due to the imperfect state of preservation of the tablet, a proper edition will require more time with the original. Subsequently, the tablet was baked and cleaned, and I was fortunate enough to obtain new photographs courtesy of Andrew George. Subsequently, in June 2009 Konrad Volk kindly collated my edition against the original.

office:text.text:p: The writing on this tablet continues uninterrupted from the obverse to the reverse, covering the lower edge. Two of the three lines on that edge (20–21) are difficult to read; additional conservation may make it all easier to decipher. The Sumerian of this letter leaves much to be desired, even more so than some of the other CKU items.

text:p.text:span: , a high military rank in early OB but unattested in Ur III. It may designate the approximate equivalent to “general,” that is Ur III
53–70. On military ranks in OB times see, most recently, D. Charpin,

The earliest attestations are found in the literary correspondence of the Isin kings Iddin-Dagan and Lipit-Eåtar (letters SEpM2–5). The sequence of military ranks does not correspond to anything we know from any specific period, and probably represents an eighteenth century garbling of early OB titles.
. On this salutation, see the commentary to ArÅ1: 10 (1).
. The reading of the beginning of the line is highly uncertain but it undoubtedly contains a personal name that begins with Åulgi.

, when used of workers/troops, means, “to muster” but here it is used with the allative case, which is unusual. The broken context does not help matters but it is possible that this has to be connected with ÅAr2: 6–7 (5)
, “Why did you remove him from that regiment and throw him into prison?”
. This line apparently also occurs in ArÅ3: 11 (6).
. The restorations in these lines are conjectural; one would expect a genitive construction with
in both lines. Either this is simply a bad rendition from the Akkadian (which underlies much of this text as it does with other letters), or it is a parenthetical interjection, literally, “
From this very [day] on, I will never neglect orders, o my king! [(Your) orders
], o my king, must never be transgressed!”
